The confusion probably comes from the way you're printing the array? If the key is indeed 'country', you should see this:

~~~js

[
  { "'country'": 'China', population: 1439000000 },
  columns: [ 'country', 'population' ]
]
~~~

can you try this?
~~~sh
   echo 'console.log({country: "China", population: 1.439})' | node
~~~

body:
No. From https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Functions/Arrow_functions:

> There are differences between arrow functions and traditional functions, as well as some limitations:
> 
> * Arrow functions don't have their own bindings to [this](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/this), [arguments](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Functions/arguments) or [super](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/super), and should not be used as [methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/Method).

body:
ES modules are already published to npm and exposed as the default export. You can access them on the CDN like so:

https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-array/src/index.js

We don’t provide a single-file ES module for each package because cross-package dependencies require bare specifiers, e.g.,

https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3/src/index.js

There isn’t really a way to publish single-file ES modules with dependencies that work out-of-the-box in web browsers on a CDN. Either users would be required to specify [import maps](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/webappapis.html#import-maps) (user burden, and unsure about browser support), or the published files would have to use relative paths or absolute URLs, which would cause problems in Node.

Given these challenges and the lack of a convention (other than the ones already used by D3), I don’t think it should be D3’s responsibility to implement bundling. If you want a pre-built bundle, I recommend using one of the existing ESM CDN bundlers such as Skypack or jsDelivr’s esm.run. Try one of these:

https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3@7/+esm
https://cdn.skypack.dev/d3@7
author: mbostock

body:
But neither of these methods provide an easy path to have the **files** needed to ship or host d3.js without the use of an extensive build system cumbersome to understand and get a build out of it. 
Neither jsdelivr nor skypack provide something I can donwload, put into my codebase (or onto my server) and give it a go, or could I? 

I've actually ended with reconverting a downloaded single file CommonJS build into ESM format again (needing some manual operations to get it working).
author: dronus

body: One service that provides bundled ES modules is esm.sh: https://esm.sh/d3@7.9.0/es2022/d3.bundle.mjs

body:
fix with patch-package `patches\d3+7.7.0.patch`

```
diff --git a/node_modules/d3/package.json b/node_modules/d3/package.json
index 104c495..6ce3f02 100644
--- a/node_modules/d3/package.json
+++ b/node_modules/d3/package.json
@@ -30,8 +30,11 @@
   "jsdelivr": "dist/d3.min.js",
   "unpkg": "dist/d3.min.js",
   "exports": {
-    "umd": "./dist/d3.min.js",
-    "default": "./src/index.js"
+    ".": {
+      "umd": "./dist/d3.min.js",
+      "default": "./src/index.js"
+    },
+    "./package.json": "./package.json"
   },
   "dependencies": {
     "d3-array": "3",

```

This would be cool to see exist for the D3 docs, for sure. I'm also investigating building something similar, but separate from D3 itself. I can't speak to whether or not this would be welcome as a contribution in this repository (I'm not a maintainer), but I'm personally interested in making something like this happen.

I have a few questions that would likely be top of mind for actually considering implementing this for D3:
 * How are you envisioning the deployment would work?
 * Where would the embeddings be stored?
 * Who would pay the OpenAI bills?
 * Would you be willing to host a separate PoC site that lives separately from the D3 docs site, just to show the path forward?
 
Thanks for the idea and the open source AI product!
author: curran

body:
I am seeing 2  security vulnerabilities in D3.js 

1) Dynamic Code injection or execution: Where random code can be executed by the attacker in the front-end using the HTML input,

2) Race Condition: In a multi-threading system, threads need to execute concurrently in some order, but the attacker may exploit the order of execution and he may get access to some data.

author: mbostock

body:
As for the other cases, this:

```js
d3.min(['5', '10', null, '2'], (d) => +d)
```

is equivalent to this:

```js
d3.min(['5', '10', null, '2'].map((d) => +d))
```

is equivalent to this:

```js
d3.min([5, 10, 0, 2])
```

hence it returns zero.

And likewise for d3.least, it doesn’t care that the _element_ is null, it cares whether the return value of the given accessor is null. So if you coerce null to zero in your accessor, and zero is the least value, then d3.least will return the corresponding element which is null.
author: mbostock

body: Yes. Thank you. I know this is the expected behavior. The problem is that the accessor is not really being used to access anything, but to coerce and change values in the dataset, which can lead to cryptic code. Mislead by an example of mine where I used +d to coerce strings, my student spent a day trying to fix a bug and fixed it after removing nulls from her dataset. She had understood that extent should ignore nulls, but wasn't aware that they were being coerced.
author: helderdarocha

body: Yes, I recommend that you apply type coercion as early as possible — ideally when the data is loaded, say by using d3.autoType in conjunction with d3.csv, or Observable’s FileAttachment helper with typed: true. If you defer type coercion until later, there is much more surface area for these problems.

author: curran

body:
This is presumably referring to the regular expression used by d3.interpolateString that finds numbers embedded within strings.

https://github.com/d3/d3-interpolate/blob/2eeffc0d02f2947552cf0a0b73bdf26fe90e1c28/src/string.js#L3

D3 is almost never used to handle user input, so these ReDoS vulnerabilities are generally a huge waste of time. But if someone wants to optimize the above regular expression to avoid catastrophic backtracking, that’d be welcome. We made similar improvements in [d3-color 3.1.0](https://github.com/d3/d3-color/releases/tag/v3.1.0).
author: mbostock

body:
To clarify, I think `µ` is latin-1, it's just `−` that's not, as far as I can tell.

For context browsers use UTF-16 and Latin-1 for compatibility reasons. In addition to being faster, as Camillo mentioned, it would also use less memory as Latin-1.

body: This happens because by default Terser (our minifier) uses [`ascii_only: false`](https://terser.org/docs/options/#format-options). We could set that to true, but we’d need to do it across the D3 code base, and it makes me wonder if it wouldn’t be more productive to file an issue with Terser to change the default.
author: mbostock

body: Thanks. Can you point to such cases where the current code fails?
author: Fil

body:
The current regex in test/docs-test.js fails when a URL contains more than one level of nested parentheses.

Here are examples of cases that fail :

Deeply Nested Parentheses:
[nested](url(foo(bar))) - The inner (bar) breaks the regex because it only accounts for one level of (...).
[complex](url(a(b(c)d)e))
Unbalanced Parentheses (if not covered by the single-level logic):
[broken](url(foo) - Fails as expected (invalid markdown link usually, but regex won't match it).
The regex ((?:[^()]|\([^()]*\))*) specifically allows for:

Non-parenthesis characters [^()]
OR a single level of balanced parentheses \([^()]*\) (which cannot contain inner parentheses).
So any URL like http://example.com/data(series(2021)) will fail to be parsed as a link.
author: Bitshifter-9

body: My question is, are any of these actual cases from our documentation?
author: Fil

body:
Yes, there are actual cases in the documentation that the original regex failed to handle correctly:

1. Nested parentheses
In docs/d3-shape/arc.md, the link
[annular](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Annulus_(mathematics))
was being truncated to:
...Annulus_(mathematics
because the old regex stopped matching at the first closing parenthesis.
The new regex correctly handles nested parentheses inside URLs.

2. Titles inside links
In docs/d3-force/simulation.md, the link
[Source]:https://github.com/d3/d3-force/blob/main/src/simulation.js
had the "Source" title incorrectly parsed as part of the URL.
The updated regex properly detects and excludes optional titles.

The new regex addresses both issues and ensures proper handling of complex markdown links.
@Fil
author: Bitshifter-9

body:
Thanks. I checked the "annular" link at https://d3js.org/d3-shape/arc#arcs and it seems to correctly include the closing paren.

However there is indeed an issue with the link at https://d3js.org/d3-force/simulation#simulation_randomSource — but it's because the markdown is malformed, fix at #4084

body: Per [this graph](https://observablehq.com/@mbostock/package-dependencies) it is a transitive dependency from d3-dsv, not a direct dependency of d3. It is used to provide the CLI for d3-dsv. I don’t have any plans to do an immediate upgrade but I’ll get to it if I can, or someone can make a contribution to d3-dsv.

body: The d3/d3.github.com check out is only used to serve old versions of D3 for backwards compatibility. For example, https://d3js.org/d3-array.v0.6.js. We don’t run tests against the old versions.
author: mbostock
